passages.explanation: Three days after arriving in the province, the new governor Festus goes up from Caesarea to Jerusalem. The chief priests and Jewish leaders immediately bring charges against Paul and request a favour — have Paul transferred to Jerusalem. They're planning an ambush to kill him on the way. Festus refuses: Paul is being held in Caesarea, and he's going back there shortly. Let your leaders come with me and press charges there. Two years later, and they're still trying the same assassination strategy. Festus unknowingly preserves Paul's life by following standard Roman procedure.

After eight to ten days in Jerusalem, Festus returns to Caesarea and convenes the court. The Jews from Jerusalem bring many serious charges they can't prove. Paul's defence is straightforward: I've done nothing wrong against the Jewish law, the temple, or Caesar. Festus, wanting to do the Jews a favour, asks Paul if he's willing to go to Jerusalem to stand trial. Paul knows what that means: I'm standing before Caesar's court, where I ought to be tried. I've done no wrong to the Jews, as you know very well. If I'm guilty of anything deserving death, I don't refuse to die. But if the charges are baseless, no one has the right to hand me over to them. I appeal to Caesar. Festus consults his council and then declares: you have appealed to Caesar — to Caesar you will go. Paul has just invoked his right as a Roman citizen to have his case heard by the emperor himself. It's the legal mechanism that will take him to Rome — exactly where the Lord said he'd go.

Some days later, King Agrippa II and his sister Bernice arrive in Caesarea to pay their respects to Festus. During their stay, Festus discusses Paul's case. He explains the situation: Felix left a prisoner, the Jews want him condemned, but Roman law doesn't hand someone over without a proper trial. When the accusers came, the charges weren't what Festus expected — no criminal matters, just disputes about their religion and about a dead man named Jesus whom Paul claims is alive. Festus admits he's at a loss how to investigate these matters, which is why he asked Paul about going to Jerusalem. Paul appealed to the Emperor instead. Agrippa says: I'd like to hear this man myself. Tomorrow, Festus says. Festus' summary is revealing — to a Roman official, the whole case comes down to one claim: whether a dead man is alive.

The next day, Agrippa and Bernice arrive with great pomp, accompanied by military commanders and prominent city leaders. Festus presents Paul and explains his dilemma: the Jewish community, both in Jerusalem and Caesarea, has demanded this man's death. But I found he's done nothing deserving death. He's appealed to the Emperor, and I've decided to send him. The problem: I have no clear charge to write. It seems unreasonable to send a prisoner to Rome without specifying the charges. That's why he's brought Paul before Agrippa — hopefully the king can help him formulate something. Festus needs Agrippa's expertise in Jewish affairs to make sense of a case that baffles Roman legal categories.
